ai-as-your-personal-assistant.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to act as your personal assistant and help you plan your upcoming week. Include your real commitments, priorities, and any constraints.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.placeholder: Help me plan my week. I have these commitments...
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.hint: The more context you give AI about your life — energy levels, preferences, fixed commitments — the better it can plan around your reality, not some ideal schedule.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.goodExample: Help me plan next week (Mon-Fri). Fixed commitments: Mon 10am team standup, Tue/Thu 2pm client calls (1hr each), Wed 9am dentist appointment (expect to be out until 11am), Fri 3pm weekly review. My top 3 priorities: finish the Q1 report (needs ~4 focused hours), prepare Thursday's presentation (2 hours), and clear my email backlog (currently 47 unread). I'm a morning person — my best focus time is 8-11am. I crash after lunch (1-2pm). I need 30 min of exercise daily. Don't schedule deep work in back-to-back days — I burn out. Build me a day-by-day schedule with specific time blocks, including breaks. Flag if anything doesn't fit.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.successMessage: A good AI assistant prompt includes your constraints, energy patterns, and priorities — not just your to-do list. Notice how the plan works around your reality instead of pretending you're a robot.

ai-as-your-personal-assistant.instruction: Describe three recurring tasks in your daily life that you could delegate to AI right now. For each, explain what you currently do manually, how AI could help, and the estimated time you'd save per week.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.placeholder: Task 1: Currently I... AI could... Time saved: ...
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.hint: Think beyond work: meal planning, email drafting, appointment scheduling, research, reading summaries, budget tracking, workout planning.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.badExample: 1. Writing emails. 2. Making lists. 3. Organizing stuff.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.goodExample:
Task 1: DAILY EMAIL TRIAGE — Currently I spend 30 minutes each morning reading through 20-30 emails, deciding what's urgent, drafting replies. AI could: summarize each email in one line, flag urgent ones, draft responses for routine requests (meeting confirmations, status updates, info requests). Time saved: 2.5 hours/week.

Task 2: WEEKLY MEAL PLANNING — Currently I spend 45 minutes Sunday browsing recipes, checking what's in the fridge, making a grocery list. AI could: generate a week of meals based on my dietary preferences and what I already have, create a consolidated shopping list organized by store aisle, and adjust portions for how many people I'm cooking for. Time saved: 35 minutes/week plus less food waste.

Task 3: MEETING PREP — Before every meeting (about 8/week), I spend 15 minutes reviewing past notes, checking the agenda, and preparing talking points. AI could: pull my notes from the last meeting, summarize the agenda, suggest questions I should ask based on my goals, and draft follow-up action items. Time saved: 1.5 hours/week.

Total: ~4.5 hours/week recovered.
ai-as-your-personal-assistant.successMessage: 4.5 hours per week is 234 hours per year — nearly six full work weeks. And those are just three tasks. The key is being specific about what you currently do manually so AI can slot in precisely where it helps most.

building-your-ai-productivity-system.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to design a personal productivity system tailored to your specific work style, challenges, and tools. Be honest about what hasn't worked for you before.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.placeholder: Design a productivity system for me. My work style is... I've tried... but...
building-your-ai-productivity-system.hint: The best system is one you'll actually use. Tell AI about past failures so it can design around your real patterns, not ideal ones.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.goodExample: Design a productivity system for me. Context: I'm a freelance designer working from home, managing 4-5 client projects simultaneously. My challenges: I have ADHD, so traditional time-blocking fails because I can't predict when hyperfocus kicks in. I've tried: Pomodoro (too rigid), GTD (too complex to maintain), and bullet journaling (forgot to use it after week 2). What DOES work: I'm great with short lists, I respond well to deadlines, and I like visual tracking. Tools I already use: Notion, Google Calendar, iPhone reminders. Design a system that: works with my variable focus patterns instead of against them, takes under 5 minutes to maintain daily, handles multi-project switching, and includes a weekly review I'll actually do. Be specific — I need the exact setup, not just principles.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.successMessage: The best productivity system is built around your brain, not someone else's ideal workflow. By sharing what hasn't worked, you've given AI the constraints it needs to design something you'll actually stick with.

building-your-ai-productivity-system.instruction: Analyze this person's daily routine and identify three productivity problems AI could solve: 'I wake up, check emails in bed for 30 minutes, rush to get ready, arrive at work stressed, spend the morning in meetings, eat lunch at my desk while working, have my most productive hours from 2-4pm but that's when I'm usually in more meetings, leave work at 6pm with unfinished tasks, and worry about them all evening.'
building-your-ai-productivity-system.hint: Look for: energy misalignment, reactive vs. proactive time, boundary issues, and tasks that could be automated or batched.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.badExample: They should wake up earlier, exercise, and stop checking email in bed.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.goodExample:
Three AI-solvable problems:

1. MORNING EMAIL TRAP — Checking email in bed starts the day in reactive mode, creating stress before they've even gotten up. AI solution: Set up an AI email summary that runs at 7am and sends a 3-line digest: 'You have 2 urgent emails (from boss and client X), 5 routine, 12 skippable.' They get the peace of mind that nothing's on fire WITHOUT the 30-minute scroll. The phone stays face-down until they're ready.

2. PEAK HOURS WASTED IN MEETINGS — Their best thinking happens 2-4pm but that's consumed by meetings. AI solution: Use AI to audit their calendar weekly and identify which meetings could be async (AI generates summary docs + action items instead), which could be shortened (AI creates tight agendas with time limits), and which should be moved to morning low-energy slots. Target: reclaim 3+ hours/week of peak time.

3. EVENING WORRY LOOP — Unfinished tasks create anxiety because there's no capture system. AI solution: At 5:45pm, a daily 'brain dump' prompt where they voice-note or type everything unfinished. AI organizes it into tomorrow's priority list with time estimates, sends it to their task manager, and generates a 'status' message: 'You completed 7 of 9 tasks today. The 2 remaining are queued for tomorrow 9am. Nothing is urgent overnight.' The clear boundary between work-brain and home-brain reduces evening anxiety.
building-your-ai-productivity-system.successMessage: Productivity problems are rarely about willpower — they're about systems. Each of these AI solutions removes a friction point that the person can't solve with 'just try harder.' That's compassionate productivity design.

creative-projects-and-hobbies.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you plan and make progress on a creative project or hobby you've been putting off. Be specific about the project, where you're stuck, and how much time you have.
creative-projects-and-hobbies.placeholder: Help me make progress on my [creative project]. I'm stuck because...
creative-projects-and-hobbies.hint: Creative blocks often come from the project feeling too big. Ask AI to break it into tiny, specific next steps you can start in under 5 minutes.
creative-projects-and-hobbies.goodExample: Help me restart my stalled novel. I wrote 15,000 words of a mystery novel 6 months ago and stopped. I have the premise (small-town detective investigating disappearances connected to a local festival) and 3 main characters, but I'm stuck on the middle — I know the ending but can't figure out how to get there. I have 30 minutes per day, usually at night after the kids are in bed. Break my 'get unstuck' process into 7 days of specific 30-minute tasks. Day 1 should be the easiest possible thing so I actually do it. Don't tell me to 'just write' — give me concrete exercises like 'write a 200-word scene where character X discovers Y' or 'list 5 possible clues that connect A to B.'
creative-projects-and-hobbies.successMessage: Creative blocks dissolve when the next step is small enough to start without thinking. AI excels at breaking overwhelming projects into bite-sized pieces — it's like having a writing coach who never judges your pace.

creative-projects-and-hobbies.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a learning plan for a new hobby or skill you want to pick up. Include your current level, available time, and how you prefer to learn.
creative-projects-and-hobbies.placeholder: Create a learning plan for [hobby/skill]. I'm a complete beginner and...
creative-projects-and-hobbies.hint: Good learning plans account for your schedule, learning style, and what 'success' looks like to you. Include constraints so AI builds something realistic.
creative-projects-and-hobbies.goodExample: Create a 6-week beginner watercolor painting plan. I've never painted before but I can draw basic sketches. I have 20 minutes on weekday mornings and 1 hour on Saturday. I learn best by doing (not watching videos). I want to be able to paint a simple landscape by the end. Budget for supplies: $50. For each week: list the one technique to focus on, a specific 20-minute daily exercise, the Saturday project that applies the technique, and what 'good enough' looks like at my level (I tend to be too perfectionist and quit). Also tell me the 3 supplies I need for week 1 so I don't over-buy and overwhelm myself.
creative-projects-and-hobbies.successMessage: The best hobby plans are built around your real life, not an imaginary schedule with unlimited time. By specifying your perfectionism tendency, you've given AI the context to set realistic expectations that keep you going.

email-and-communication.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to draft a difficult email you've been avoiding. Give the full context: who it's to, the situation, what you need to communicate, and the tone you want.
email-and-communication.placeholder: Draft an email to [person] about [difficult situation]...
email-and-communication.hint: Difficult emails are hard because of emotional complexity, not writing complexity. Give AI the backstory so it can navigate the tone perfectly.
email-and-communication.goodExample: Draft an email to my manager asking to switch from a project I was excited about 2 months ago but now realize is a poor fit for my skills. Context: She specifically chose me for this project and announced it to the team. I've been struggling silently for 6 weeks. The project needs someone with strong data engineering skills (which I don't have) — I'm a frontend developer. I'm not trying to avoid work; I think the project would go better with someone else AND I could contribute more on the UI redesign that's currently understaffed. Tone: honest, professional, solution-oriented (not whiny or defeatist). Acknowledge her trust in choosing me. Under 200 words. Include a suggested subject line that won't alarm her.
email-and-communication.successMessage: The hardest emails to write are the ones where we're vulnerable. AI can draft the words while you provide the emotional intelligence about the relationship. Notice how the prompt's backstory ensures the email navigates the politics correctly.

email-and-communication.instruction: Rewrite this passive-aggressive email to be direct but kind: 'As per my previous email (which I sent on March 3rd and March 10th), the report was due last Friday. I'm sure you've been very busy, but we really need this ASAP as others are waiting. Thanks so much!!'
email-and-communication.placeholder: A direct but kind version would be...
email-and-communication.hint: Remove the sarcasm markers ('as per my previous email', 'I'm sure you've been very busy', double exclamation marks) and replace with clear, warm directness.

mustNotInclude: I'm sure you've been very busy

email-and-communication.badExample: Send the report now. It was due Friday. This is the third time I'm asking.
email-and-communication.goodExample: Hi Jordan — I'm following up on the Q1 report that was due last Friday. I know things come up, but our team is blocked until we have it — Sarah needs the data for her client presentation Wednesday. Can you send it by end of day tomorrow? If something's preventing you from finishing it, let me know and I can help or we can adjust the timeline. Thanks.
email-and-communication.successMessage: Direct and kind beats passive-aggressive every time. The rewrite states the problem clearly, explains the impact on others, gives a specific deadline, and offers help instead of guilt. That's communication that gets results without damaging relationships.

financial-planning-basics.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you create a realistic monthly budget. Include your income, major expense categories, and financial goals.
financial-planning-basics.placeholder: Help me create a monthly budget. I earn... My major expenses are...
financial-planning-basics.hint: Be honest about spending. AI can only help if you give it real numbers. Include both fixed and variable expenses, plus what you want to save for.
financial-planning-basics.goodExample: Help me create a realistic monthly budget. Take-home pay: $4,200/month. Fixed expenses: rent $1,350, car payment $280, insurance (car + renter's) $165, phone $85, student loans $220, subscriptions (streaming, gym, Spotify) $65. Variable: groceries $400-500, gas $120-160, eating out $200-300 (this is my weakness), miscellaneous $100-200. Goals: build a $5,000 emergency fund (currently at $800), start saving for a vacation ($2,000 by August), and stop overdrafting (happened twice last quarter). Create a budget that's realistic for someone who's tried and failed with strict budgets before. Include a 'guilt-free spending' category so I don't feel deprived. Show me exactly how long it'll take to hit each goal.
financial-planning-basics.successMessage: A budget that acknowledges your spending personality (including the 'guilt-free' category) is one you'll actually follow. AI helped you see the math clearly without judgment — now you know exactly what's possible and when.

financial-planning-basics.instruction: Analyze this spending pattern and identify the biggest opportunity for saving: 'I spend about $15/day on lunch, $6/day on coffee, $200/month on subscriptions I mostly use, $150/month on impulse Amazon purchases, and $100/month on unused gym membership.' Which expense should be tackled first and why?
financial-planning-basics.placeholder: The biggest saving opportunity is... because...
financial-planning-basics.hint: Don't just pick the biggest number. Consider: ease of change, recurring vs. one-time, emotional attachment, and actual usage. The best target is high impact AND easy to change.
financial-planning-basics.goodExample:
Ranked by impact AND ease of change:

1. UNUSED GYM MEMBERSHIP ($100/mo = $1,200/year) — Tackle first. It's the easiest win because there's zero lifestyle sacrifice: you're already not using it. Cancel today. If you want to exercise, start with free options (walking, YouTube workouts) and only rejoin a gym when you've built a consistent habit. Savings: $1,200/year with zero pain.

2. IMPULSE AMAZON PURCHASES ($150/mo = $1,800/year) — Second target. Implement a '48-hour cart rule': add items to cart but don't buy for 48 hours. Studies show 70% of impulse purchases aren't completed after a cooling period. Install a browser extension that shows cart total. Expected savings: $900-1,200/year.

3. SUBSCRIPTIONS ($200/mo) — Audit, don't cancel everything. 'Mostly use' means some are unused. Cancel the bottom 3 you haven't opened in 30 days. Expected savings: $40-80/month.

4. DAILY LUNCH ($15/day = $330/mo) — Pack lunch 3 days/week instead of 5 to keep it sustainable. Savings: $135/month.

5. COFFEE ($6/day = $132/mo) — The classic advice to 'cut coffee' is bad advice if it's your daily joy. Maybe switch to home brew 2-3 days/week. Savings: $50-70/month.

Total achievable savings: $400-550/month without feeling deprived. That's your emergency fund fully funded in 8-10 months.
financial-planning-basics.successMessage: The gym membership is the classic 'invisible expense' — money leaving your account for nothing. Starting with painless wins builds momentum for the harder changes. Notice how the analysis respects your quality of life instead of demanding austerity.

meal-planning-and-health.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a week of meal plans based on your dietary needs, cooking skill, time constraints, and budget. Be specific about your preferences and limitations.
meal-planning-and-health.placeholder: Create a meal plan for the week. I eat... I have... minutes to cook...
meal-planning-and-health.hint: Include everything: dietary restrictions, cooking skill, kitchen equipment, grocery budget, and how many people you're feeding. Also mention foods you hate.
meal-planning-and-health.goodExample: Create a 5-day dinner meal plan (Mon-Fri) for 2 adults. Constraints: I'm lactose intolerant, partner is vegetarian (eats eggs), weekly grocery budget $80 for dinners only. Cooking skill: intermediate (comfortable with stir-fry, pasta, baking — but nothing that takes more than 40 minutes on weeknights). Kitchen: stovetop, oven, Instant Pot, no air fryer. Preferences: love Thai, Mexican, and Italian flavors. Hate: mushrooms, tofu (texture issue), bell peppers. Additional: make Monday's recipe produce enough leftovers for Tuesday's lunch. Include a consolidated grocery list organized by store section (produce, pantry, frozen, etc.) and flag any ingredients that can be used in multiple recipes to reduce waste.
meal-planning-and-health.successMessage: This prompt gives AI everything it needs to create meals you'll actually cook and enjoy. The consolidated grocery list and ingredient reuse across recipes are pro moves that save money and reduce food waste.

meal-planning-and-health.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you build a sustainable health habit. Choose something specific (hydration, sleep, movement, etc.) and ask for a gradual plan that accounts for your current reality.
meal-planning-and-health.placeholder: Help me build a habit of [health goal]. Currently I...
meal-planning-and-health.hint: Sustainable habits start tiny and grow. Ask AI to design a ramp-up that's so easy you can't say no to day 1.
meal-planning-and-health.goodExample: Help me build a consistent sleep routine. Currently: I fall asleep between 11pm-2am depending on my phone scrolling, wake up groggy at 7:30am, hit snooze 3-4 times, and need coffee before I'm functional. I've tried: setting a phone alarm (I ignore it), going cold turkey on screens (lasted 2 days), melatonin (made me groggy the next day). What works for me: gradual changes, visual tracking (I love filling in habit tracker boxes), and having a specific sequence to follow (I'm good with routines once they exist). Design a 4-week ramp-up plan where Week 1 is ridiculously easy and each week adds one small change. Include what to do when I inevitably fail a night (because 'start over from Week 1' kills my motivation).
meal-planning-and-health.successMessage: The best health habits are built on self-knowledge, not willpower. By sharing your past failures and what actually motivates you, you've given AI the blueprint for a plan that works with your brain instead of against it.

morning-routines-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to design a morning routine that fits your real life. Include your wake-up time, morning obligations, energy patterns, and what you've tried before.
morning-routines-with-ai.placeholder: Design a morning routine for me. I wake up at... I need to...
morning-routines-with-ai.hint: Morning routines fail when they're copied from someone else's life. Give AI YOUR constraints — kids, commute, energy levels, non-negotiables.
morning-routines-with-ai.badExample: Give me a morning routine like a CEO.
morning-routines-with-ai.goodExample: Design a realistic morning routine for me. I wake up at 6:15am, need to leave for work by 7:30am. Non-negotiables in that 75 minutes: shower, get dressed, feed the dog, pack lunch (if I didn't the night before), and drive my kid to school by 7:15am (takes 10 min round trip). Current reality: I wake up, immediately check my phone for 15 minutes, rush through everything, skip breakfast, forget things, and arrive at work frazzled. I want: to feel calm before work, eat something (even small), and have 10 minutes of quiet before the rush. I am NOT a morning person — don't give me a 5am wake-up or meditation. Design a minute-by-minute timeline that fits everything and identify what I should prep the night before to buy myself time in the morning.
morning-routines-with-ai.successMessage: A morning routine that fits in 75 minutes with a kid, a dog, and a commute is a logistics puzzle — and AI just solved it. The night-before prep is the secret weapon that buys you those calm minutes in the morning.

morning-routines-with-ai.instruction: List three ways you could use AI each morning to reduce decision fatigue before your day even starts. For each, describe the specific AI prompt or automation you'd set up.
morning-routines-with-ai.placeholder: 1. AI morning briefing that... 2. Outfit/meal decision... 3. Priority setting...
morning-routines-with-ai.hint: Decision fatigue is real — every small choice in the morning costs mental energy. Think about: what to wear, what to eat, what to work on first, what emails need attention.
morning-routines-with-ai.badExample: 1. AI tells me what to do. 2. AI picks my clothes. 3. AI makes breakfast.
morning-routines-with-ai.goodExample:
Three AI Morning Decision Eliminators:

1. AI DAILY BRIEFING (5:55am auto-email): A prompt that runs nightly and emails me a morning brief: today's calendar at a glance, weather + clothing suggestion (based on my wardrobe preferences), the ONE priority I should tackle first (pulled from yesterday's end-of-day brain dump), and any emails that arrived overnight needing morning attention. I read this instead of mindlessly scrolling.

2. AI MEAL DECIDER: A weekly prompt where I input what's in my fridge on Sunday. AI generates 5 breakfasts and 5 lunches, mapped to each day. Monday morning I don't think — I just look at the plan. The prompt accounts for prep time (overnight oats Sunday = grab-and-go Monday) and uses ingredients before they expire.

3. AI FOCUS SETTER: Before I open my laptop, I tell AI: 'Here's my calendar today and my task list. If I can only do 3 things before noon, which 3 move the needle most? Rank by: impact, urgency, and what's blocking others.' This prevents the morning trap of doing easy tasks first while important ones wait.
morning-routines-with-ai.successMessage: Decision fatigue is the silent productivity killer. By offloading small choices to AI before your day starts, you preserve mental energy for the decisions that actually matter. These three automations could transform your mornings.

research-and-learning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you understand a complex topic quickly. Choose something you genuinely want to learn about and specify your current knowledge level and how much time you have.
research-and-learning.placeholder: Explain [complex topic] to me. I currently know... I have [time]...
research-and-learning.hint: The best learning prompts tell AI your existing knowledge so it doesn't waste time on what you already know, and your time constraint so it prioritizes ruthlessly.
research-and-learning.goodExample: Explain how CRISPR gene editing works. My level: I have a college biology background (took genetics 10 years ago), I understand DNA basics and mutations, but I don't know anything about the CRISPR mechanism specifically. I have 15 minutes. Structure this as: (1) The 2-sentence 'cocktail party explanation' I can tell anyone, (2) The 5-minute version with an analogy that makes the mechanism click, (3) The key limitation or controversy I should know about, (4) Three follow-up questions I could explore if I want to go deeper. Skip: history of discovery, researcher names, anything I'd need a PhD to appreciate. I want to understand how it works, not its backstory.
research-and-learning.successMessage: This prompt respects your time AND your existing knowledge. The tiered explanation (2-sentence, 5-minute, deeper) means you get exactly the depth you need. AI just compressed a college lecture into 15 minutes of targeted learning.

research-and-learning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a reading list or learning path for a skill you want to develop. Specify the skill, your goal level, available time per week, and preferred learning formats.
research-and-learning.placeholder: Create a learning path for [skill]. I want to reach [level] in [timeframe]...
research-and-learning.hint: Ask AI to sequence the resources so each builds on the last. Include preferred formats (books, videos, podcasts, hands-on practice) and time constraints.
research-and-learning.badExample: Give me resources to learn Python.
research-and-learning.goodExample: Create a 12-week learning path for SQL. Goal: go from zero to being able to write complex queries (JOINs, subqueries, window functions) well enough to pull my own data at work instead of asking our data team. Available time: 3 hours/week (1 hour weeknight x2 + 1 hour Saturday). Learning preferences: I learn by doing, not watching. Short articles over long textbooks. I need free resources only. I use PostgreSQL at work. Structure as: weekly themes that build on each other, one core resource per week (the must-do), one practice exercise using real-world data, and a mini-project every 4 weeks to validate progress. Include a 'skip to here' checkpoint for each month in case I fall behind — I'd rather skip ahead than give up.
research-and-learning.successMessage: A learning path with skip-ahead checkpoints is brilliant for real life — because nobody follows a 12-week plan perfectly. You've built in resilience so a bad week doesn't derail the whole journey.

task-management-and-prioritization.instruction: Write a prompt giving AI a brain dump of everything on your plate and asking it to prioritize ruthlessly. Include at least 8 tasks of varying importance and urgency.
task-management-and-prioritization.placeholder: Here's everything I need to do: [list]. Help me prioritize...
task-management-and-prioritization.hint: A good brain dump includes deadlines, stakes, dependencies, and estimated effort. Let AI sort the chaos — that's exactly what it's for.
task-management-and-prioritization.badExample: I have a lot to do. Help me prioritize.
task-management-and-prioritization.goodExample:
Brain dump — help me prioritize. Today is Monday, I have 6 productive hours:
1. Respond to client email about contract changes (they sent it Friday, expecting reply today)
2. Prepare slides for Wednesday team presentation (needs ~2 hours)
3. Review 3 job applications for our open role (hiring manager wants feedback by Thursday)
4. Fix the bug in the checkout flow (customers complaining, no workaround)
5. Schedule dentist appointment (been putting this off for 3 months)
6. Write monthly newsletter (goes out Thursday, haven't started)
7. Organize my desktop files (bothering me but not urgent)
8. Call Mom back (she called twice this weekend)
9. Research new project management tools (boss asked casually, no deadline)
10. Update my LinkedIn profile (been meaning to for weeks)

For each: classify as Do Today / Do This Week / Schedule Later / Delegate or Drop. Then give me a time-blocked plan for today's 6 hours. Factor in that my focus is best 9-11am, I get sluggish after lunch, and I need to leave by 5pm.
task-management-and-prioritization.successMessage: A brain dump only works when you get EVERYTHING out of your head — even the small stuff. AI prioritization removes the emotional weight of deciding and gives you a clear execution plan. The checkout bug and client email are obviously urgent, but notice how AI also scheduled the dentist — that's the kind of task that slides forever without a system.

task-management-and-prioritization.instruction: Analyze what's wrong with this to-do list and explain how to fix it: '1. Work on project. 2. Exercise. 3. Emails. 4. Be more organized. 5. Read more.' Why will this list never get done?
task-management-and-prioritization.placeholder: This list fails because... Better versions would be...
task-management-and-prioritization.hint: Effective tasks are specific, actionable, time-bound, and small enough to start without thinking. This list is vague, open-ended, and has no completion criteria.
task-management-and-prioritization.badExample: The tasks need to be more specific.
task-management-and-prioritization.goodExample:
This list will never get done because every item is a category, not a task. The problems:

1. 'Work on project' — Which project? What specifically? When is it done? You'll open the file, stare at it, feel overwhelmed, and switch to email. FIX: 'Write the intro section of the Q1 report (aim for 300 words) by 11am.'

2. 'Exercise' — No time, no type, no duration. It'll get pushed to 'later' all day. FIX: 'Walk around the block at 12:15pm (15 min, right after lunch).'

3. 'Emails' — This is a black hole, not a task. You'll spend 2 hours and still feel behind. FIX: 'Reply to the 3 starred emails from clients by 10am. Process inbox to under 20 unread by 4pm.'

4. 'Be more organized' — This isn't even a task, it's an identity aspiration. You can't cross it off. FIX: 'Spend 15 minutes filing last week's documents into the shared drive.'

5. 'Read more' — Same problem. When? What? How much? FIX: 'Read 20 pages of [book title] before bed tonight.'

The pattern: every effective task answers WHAT specifically, WHEN, and HOW WILL I KNOW IT'S DONE.
task-management-and-prioritization.successMessage: Vague tasks create guilt. Specific tasks create momentum. The transformation from 'exercise' to 'walk around the block at 12:15' is the difference between a wish and an action. AI can convert any brain dump into actionable, time-boxed tasks.

travel-and-event-planning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to plan a trip with specific constraints. Include your destination (or ask for suggestions), budget, dates, travel style, and any special requirements.
travel-and-event-planning.placeholder: Plan a trip to [destination] for [number] people. Budget: $... Dates:...
travel-and-event-planning.hint: The more constraints you give AI, the more useful the plan. Include: group dynamics, must-see vs. nice-to-have, dietary needs, mobility considerations, and what kind of experience you want.
travel-and-event-planning.goodExample: Plan a 4-day trip to Portland, Oregon for me and my partner, arriving Thursday evening, leaving Monday morning. Budget: $1,500 total (including hotel, food, activities — flights already booked). We love: craft coffee shops, bookstores, hiking (moderate difficulty, under 5 miles), and local food (we're vegetarian). We hate: tourist traps, rushed itineraries, and waking up before 9am. Hotel preference: walkable neighborhood, under $180/night, bonus if it's a boutique hotel. Build a day-by-day plan with: morning, afternoon, and evening blocks (leave free time — we don't want every minute scheduled). Include specific restaurant names with vegetarian highlights, one scenic hike with trail details, and a rainy-day backup plan (it's Portland, after all). Estimate costs for each day.
travel-and-event-planning.successMessage: This prompt creates a trip plan that matches YOUR travel style, not a generic guidebook itinerary. The key details — vegetarian, no early mornings, walkable, rainy-day backup — prevent every common trip frustration before it happens.

travel-and-event-planning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you plan an event (birthday party, dinner gathering, team outing, etc.). Include the occasion, number of guests, budget, venue constraints, and any special considerations.
travel-and-event-planning.placeholder: Help me plan a [event type] for [number] people. Budget: $...
travel-and-event-planning.hint: Event planning has a million small decisions. Let AI handle the logistics so you can focus on the personal touches.
travel-and-event-planning.goodExample: Help me plan my best friend's 30th birthday party. Details: 25 guests, budget $600 (I'm covering food and decorations, guests bring drinks), Saturday evening 6-10pm, hosting at my apartment (open floor plan, fits ~30 people comfortably, small balcony). The birthday person loves: 90s nostalgia, karaoke, and tacos. Dietary needs in the group: 2 vegetarians, 1 gluten-free, 1 nut allergy. I need: (1) a taco bar menu that handles all dietary needs with estimated quantities and costs, (2) a simple decoration plan under $80 (90s theme), (3) a playlist structure (not specific songs — just the vibe for each phase of the evening), (4) a timeline for the evening with one group activity beyond karaoke, (5) a day-before and day-of prep checklist so I'm not scrambling. I'm organized but tend to over-plan and stress — help me keep it fun and simple.
travel-and-event-planning.successMessage: Event planning stress usually comes from trying to hold all the details in your head. You've handed the logistics to AI while keeping the personal knowledge (the 90s nostalgia, the dietary needs) that makes the party special. That's the perfect AI-human collaboration.
